messages.text: Another loss to Donovan/Grant on C-2020-0312. Judge hammered us on trade secret definition again.
Feels like we're just cursed in N.D. Cal. with Ames. But look at the order: she basically copies Vertex's 'reasonable particularity' language verbatim.
We also got dinged on spoliation again. PX-031 late production gave Donovan an easy adverse inference ask.
I keep wondering if our briefs are just too long. Donovan's are half the length and the judges seem to appreciate it.

We pulled page counts for the last 15 MSJs and I don't see a strong correlation between length and outcome. The common factor in the Donovan/Grant cases is spoliation + mushy trade secret definitions.

Quick cut across all trade secret matters: when we define 3-5 specific documents as the secret and have clean preservation, our win rate is >60%. When we say 'the platform as a whole' and have spoliation noise, it drops below 20%.
Kerr granted full SJ for Donovan. Order leans hard on inconsistencies between our 30(b)(6) testimony and PX-010 revision history.
Pulled the depo segments: Donovan spent 40+ pages just locking in that we can't point to a single document as 'the' trade secret.
We told the client the fab as a whole was the secret. That framing might be killing us.
Jury came back mostly for us on C-2021-0904. Rivera's instructions tracked Vertex but she bought our narrow definition anchored in PX-018.
Notably, no spoliation issues and our damages expert survived Daubert. Pattern holds compared to the Donovan cases.
C-2018-0220 depo yesterday was rough. Jonas kept drifting back to 'the whole modeling stack' instead of the 2-3 biddable strategies we actually care about.
Flagging for future matters: we should train witnesses on tying secrets to specific artifacts (e.g., PX-012 table, feature code) before sending them into the room with Grant.
Verdict in C-2019-2044: solid plaintiff win. Monroe's instructions framed the secret as the three heuristics in PX-004 plus parameter ranges, exactly how Emily described it.
Also worth noting: no spoliation fights, clean logs, and both experts survived Daubert. Classic 'we did the basics right' case.
Jury in C-2020-1450 split the baby. We held the line on independent development but they still hit us for some damages based on PX-045 cohorts.
Pattern looks similar to other Donovan matters: they don't win on 'everything is a secret', they win where our data is patchy and the damages story is fuzzy.
SJ order in C-2019-0113 is a mixed bag: court trimmed our overbroad theories but let the Appendix A weights go to trial.
This is what we keep seeing when we define 3-5 concrete artifacts as secrets instead of 'the platform' -- judges will narrow but not kill the case.
Full SJ for Donovan again in C-2019-0666. Park basically said we never got past 'the ad engine as a whole' and the missing auction logs didn't help.
C-2020-0599 SJ order slots cleanly into the Donovan pattern: vague secret definitions + incomplete tick data = court tracks Vertex/Helios and tosses the case.
Kerr's order in C-2023-0310 is brutal on the spoliation. He let two recipe sets survive but the rest is out because we don't have calibration logs.
